title: Emergent population dynamics of random walkers with cooperative reproduction and spatial selection
abstract: We extend the $N$ branching Brownian motions model of population invasion to higher-order asexual reproduction. Increasing reproduction order leads to qualitative changes: invasion fronts generically cease to exist beyond binary reproduction; and in the binary case itself, their speed becomes diffusion-independent. Ternary reproduction shows critical behavior, with collapse into a strongly localized `invasion bullet' in the supercritical regime, diffusive spreading in the subcritical regime, and a continuous family of fronts at criticality. These results suggest that the dominance of division and binary reproduction in nature reflects fundamental constraints on invasion dynamics.
summary: This paper explores how the complexity of reproduction rules changes the way populations spread through space. It identifies a phase transition in ternary reproduction where populations can either spread or collapse into localized 'bullets,' suggesting that the prevalence of binary division in nature may be due to fundamental dynamical constraints.
main_problem: The study investigates how higher-order asexual reproduction (beyond binary division) affects the population dynamics and existence of traveling wave solutions in invading species.
main_result: Increasing reproduction order $k$ qualitatively changes invasion dynamics: $k=2$ leads to $D$-independent wave speeds, $k=3$ exhibits a phase transition between diffusive spreading and population collapse (invasion bullets), and $k>3$ results in the disappearance of traveling waves.
method: The authors use a combination of hydrodynamic (PDE) modeling, dimensional analysis, Monte Carlo simulations of a discrete lattice model, and a matching procedure for self-similar solutions.
model_or_system: A 1D lattice model of $N$ random walkers with $k$-order cooperative reproduction ($kA 	o (k+1)A$) and spatial selection, where the leftmost particle is removed to maintain constant population size.
key_observables: Traveling wave speed ($c$), population density profiles ($u(x,t)$), characteristic width ($\sigma$), and the dimensionless parameter $\alpha = \Lambda/D$.
important_parameters: Reproduction order $k$, branching rate $\Lambda$, diffusion constant $D$, and the critical threshold $\alpha_c \simeq 7.089$ for the ternary case.
main_assumptions: The continuum approximation assumes the density profile spans many lattice sites; the hydrodynamic limit assumes $N 	o \infty$.
figures_summary: The notes mention a comparison of discrete mean-field theory and continuum solvers, and a figure (S1) comparing models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the $N$-BBM model extension, derives the hydrodynamic limit, analyzes different reproduction orders ($k=1, 2, 3, >3$), explores the critical behavior and self-similar collapse for $k=3$, and validates results via Monte Carlo simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Binary reproduction (k = 2): rescaled TWS U(ξ). Red solid line: numerical solution of Eq. (7), obeying Eq. (2). Black dashed line: numerical solution of Eqs. (6) and (2) at long times. Blue circles: MC simulation for D = 105 and N =104 at t≃2.25×104. Inset: front position L(t) vs. time. All three methods yield c≃0.43.

FIG. 3. Ternary reproduction (k = 3). Solid lines: HD numerics [23]; symbols: MC simulations, unless noted otherwise. (a, b) α = αc: TWSs. (a) u(x, t) vs. x at five equally spaced times from t = 0 to t ≃475 (αc ≃7.089, D = 1). Inset: rescaled TWS U(ξ), see Eq. (9) (black), the HD solution at late times [Eq. (11), red dashed], and MC (blue circles). (b) TWS speed selection: c vs. 1/σIC from HD runs with gamma, Gaussian, half-sine, and stretched-exponential initial conditions. Dashed line: linear fit c ∝1/σIC. Inset: σTWS vs. σIC (see text). (c) α < αc (α = 5): diffusive spreading. Shown is u(x, t) vs. x at six log-spaced times from t ≃0.1 to t ≃2 (MC: N = 500). Inset: σ2[u] vs. t (log-log);...

FIG. 4. Quaternary reproduction (k = 4): diffusive spreading in HD numerics (solid lines) and MC simulations (symbols, N = 30, 5000 replicates). u(x, t) for D = 225 (ℓ/h = 5) at eight log-spaced times from t = 0.1 to t = 10. Inset: σ2[u] vs. t approaching the diffusive scaling ∝t (dashed line).
